title: The repair shop that refuses to throw anything away
summary: Describe a specific repair shop or workshop you have visited and argue what we lose, both economically and ethically, when fewer things are repaired.
topic: What a repair shop preserves that a discount store cannot
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) describing a specific repair shop you have visited and arguing what we lose when fewer things are repaired. Avoid slogans. Use the present perfect for accumulated observation, second conditional, a relative clause, and 'however' or 'although'.
hintStarters: On the third floor of an electronics market in Seoul there is a small repair shop where a man in his sixties has been bringing dead radios back to life for almost thirty years…
If we replaced every broken appliance instead of fixing it, the calculation that would actually be lost is not only economic, but…
Although it is easy to celebrate repair as a moral activity, what I have come to think it really preserves is more specific and harder to measure, which is…

hintVocab.meaningVi: sự lỗi thời được lên kế hoạch

hintVocab.phrase: to bring something back to life

sampleText:
On the third floor of the Yongsan electronics market in Seoul there is a small repair shop where a man called Mr Lee has been bringing dead radios, amplifiers, and old cassette players back to life for almost thirty years. The shop is two metres wide. The shelves behind him are full of small drawers containing parts that have not been manufactured since the late 1990s. When I brought him a 1986 Japanese cassette deck that I had bought broken at a second-hand store, he opened it on his bench, sniffed the interior, ran a finger along a row of capacitors, and quoted me a repair price that was a fraction of what I had paid for the device itself. He did not look at a manual. He had clearly seen this particular model many times. The repair took four days. The deck has worked perfectly for two years since.

What I have come to understand from spending afternoons in repair shops like Mr Lee's is that what they preserve is not really a few old machines. It is the knowledge required to diagnose them — and that knowledge cannot be recovered once the people carrying it stop being asked to use it. Mr Lee learned to fix audio equipment from his uncle in the early 1990s, and his uncle learned it from a man who had repaired military radios in the 1960s. Each generation kept the previous generation's knowledge alive by needing to. If we replaced every broken appliance with a new one instead of fixing it, the calculation that would actually be lost is not only economic. We would also be quietly closing down a chain of practical knowledge that took half a century to build and that no manual could fully record. Although recycling programmes preserve materials, they do not preserve the ability to understand what is in a box.

However, I do not want to pretend that repair is automatically virtuous or that every broken thing should be saved. Some appliances are not worth repairing; some are dangerous to repair; some were designed in ways that make repair impossible without specialised tools that only the manufacturer owns. What I would argue is more specific. A society that has at least a few repair shops on every floor of its electronics markets — places where a faulty cassette deck or a broken kettle can be opened and looked at — is a society that still has the option of repair. A society that has lost those shops cannot make the choice anymore, even when it would prefer to. The decision is being made for us, slowly, by what we throw away. The shop on the third floor in Seoul is one of perhaps twenty left in the city. When it closes, what closes with it will not show up on any inventory.

sampleAnnotations.phrase: Each generation kept the previous generation's knowledge alive by needing to

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional for hypothetical present consequence
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If we replaced every broken appliance with a new one instead of fixing it, the calculation that would actually be lost is not only economic' dùng điều kiện loại 2 — quá khứ đơn trong mệnh đề if + 'would' trong mệnh đề chính — để bàn luận giả thuyết trái với thực tế hiện tại.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If we replaced every broken appliance with a new one instead of fixing it, the calculation that would actually be lost is not only economic' uses the second conditional — past simple in the if-clause + 'would' in the main clause — to discuss a hypothesis contrary to current reality.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect continuous for an ongoing activity from past to present
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Mr Lee has been bringing dead radios… back to life for almost thirty years' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành tiếp diễn ('have/has been + -ing') để diễn đạt một hoạt động bắt đầu trong quá khứ, vẫn đang tiếp tục đến hiện tại. Khác với hiện tại hoàn thành đơn — dạng tiếp diễn nhấn mạnh tính liên tục và quá trình.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Mr Lee has been bringing dead radios… back to life for almost thirty years' uses the present perfect continuous ('have/has been + -ing') for an activity that started in the past and continues to the present. Unlike the simple present perfect, the continuous form emphasises duration and ongoing process.

sampleTranslationVi:
Trên tầng ba của khu chợ điện tử Yongsan ở Seoul có một tiệm sửa chữa nhỏ nơi một người đàn ông tên Lee đã làm sống lại các radio chết, amply, và máy nghe băng cũ gần ba mươi năm. Tiệm rộng hai mét. Các kệ phía sau ông đầy những ngăn nhỏ chứa các bộ phận đã không còn được sản xuất từ cuối những năm 1990. Khi tôi mang đến cho ông một máy nghe băng Nhật Bản năm 1986 mà tôi đã mua hỏng ở một cửa hàng đồ cũ, ông mở nó ra trên bàn làm việc, ngửi bên trong, lướt ngón tay dọc theo một hàng tụ điện, và báo giá sửa chữa chỉ bằng một phần nhỏ so với cái tôi đã trả cho chính thiết bị đó. Ông không nhìn vào một cuốn hướng dẫn nào. Ông rõ ràng đã thấy mẫu máy cụ thể này nhiều lần. Việc sửa mất bốn ngày. Máy nghe băng đã hoạt động hoàn hảo trong hai năm kể từ đó.

Điều tôi đã dần hiểu từ việc dành các buổi chiều trong các tiệm sửa chữa như của ông Lee là điều họ bảo tồn không thực sự là vài cái máy cũ. Đó là kiến thức cần để chẩn đoán chúng — và kiến thức đó không thể phục hồi một khi những người mang nó không còn được yêu cầu sử dụng nó. Ông Lee học sửa thiết bị âm thanh từ chú của mình vào đầu những năm 1990, và chú ông học nó từ một người đã sửa radio quân sự vào những năm 1960. Mỗi thế hệ giữ kiến thức của thế hệ trước còn sống bằng cách cần nó. Nếu chúng ta thay mọi thiết bị hỏng bằng cái mới thay vì sửa nó, phép tính thực sự bị mất không chỉ là kinh tế. Chúng ta cũng sẽ âm thầm đóng cửa một chuỗi kiến thức thực tiễn mất nửa thế kỷ để xây dựng và không cuốn hướng dẫn nào có thể ghi lại trọn vẹn. Mặc dù các chương trình tái chế bảo tồn vật liệu, chúng không bảo tồn khả năng hiểu cái gì có trong một chiếc hộp.

Tuy nhiên, tôi không muốn giả vờ rằng sửa chữa tự động là đức hạnh hay rằng mọi thứ hỏng đều nên được cứu. Một số thiết bị không đáng sửa; một số nguy hiểm khi sửa; một số được thiết kế theo cách khiến việc sửa chữa không thể nếu không có dụng cụ chuyên dụng mà chỉ nhà sản xuất sở hữu. Điều tôi sẽ lập luận cụ thể hơn. Một xã hội có ít nhất vài tiệm sửa chữa trên mỗi tầng của các khu chợ điện tử — những nơi mà một máy nghe băng hỏng hay một ấm điện hỏng có thể được mở ra và xem xét — là một xã hội vẫn còn lựa chọn sửa chữa. Một xã hội đã mất những tiệm đó không thể đưa ra lựa chọn nữa, ngay cả khi nó muốn. Quyết định đang được đưa ra cho chúng ta, chậm rãi, bởi những gì chúng ta vứt đi. Tiệm trên tầng ba ở Seoul là một trong khoảng hai mươi tiệm còn lại trong thành phố. Khi nó đóng cửa, điều đóng cửa cùng nó sẽ không xuất hiện trên bất kỳ bảng kiểm kê nào.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect continuous correctly?
options: Mr Lee have been bringing dead radios back to life for almost thirty years.
Mr Lee has been bringing dead radios back to life for almost thirty years.
Mr Lee has been bring dead radios back to life for almost thirty years.
Mr Lee was bringing dead radios back to life for almost thirty years now.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect continuous = 'has/have been + -ing'; 'Mr Lee' is singular, so 'has'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the second conditional correctly?
options: If we replace every broken appliance with a new one, the calculation lost would not only be economic.
If we replaced every broken appliance with a new one, the calculation lost will not only be economic.
If we replaced every broken appliance with a new one, the calculation lost would not only be economic.
If we replaced every broken appliance with a new one, the calculation lost would not only being economic.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional: past simple in the if-clause + 'would + base verb' in the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the past perfect correctly?
options: I brought him a 1986 Japanese cassette deck that I has bought broken at a second-hand store.
I brought him a 1986 Japanese cassette deck that I had buy broken at a second-hand store.
I brought him a 1986 Japanese cassette deck that I had been buy broken at a second-hand store.
I brought him a 1986 Japanese cassette deck that I had bought broken at a second-hand store.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect = 'had + past participle'; past participle of 'buy' is 'bought'.

options: Although recycling programmes preserve materials, they do not preserve the ability to understand what is in a box.
Although recycling programmes preserve materials, but they do not preserve the ability to understand what is in a box.
Although, recycling programmes preserve materials they do not preserve the ability to understand what is in a box.
Recycling programmes preserve materials, although however they do not preserve the ability to understand what is in a box.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but', no comma after 'although', no doubling.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the present perfect passive correctly?
options: Parts that have not be manufactured since the late 1990s.
Parts that have not been manufactured since the late 1990s.
Parts that has not been manufactured since the late 1990s.
Parts that have not been manufacturing since the late 1990s.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect passive = 'have/has been + past participle': 'have not been manufactured'. Subject 'parts' is plural.
mcQuestions.hint: Present perfect passive = 'have been + past participle'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'once' correctly to mean 'as soon as'?
options: That knowledge cannot be recovered once that the people carrying it stop being asked to use it.
That knowledge cannot be recovered once when the people carrying it stop being asked to use it.
That knowledge cannot be recovered once the people carrying it stop being asked to use it.
That knowledge cannot be recovered once the people carrying it will stop being asked to use it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Once + clause' (no 'that' or 'when'); future reference in a time clause uses the present, not 'will'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Once + clause' (no 'that' / 'when'); present, not 'will', in time clauses.

options: If we replaced every broken appliance with a new one instead to fix it.
If we replaced every broken appliance with a new one instead for fixing it.
If we replaced every broken appliance with a new one in instead of fix it.
If we replaced every broken appliance with a new one instead of fixing it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Instead of + -ing form' is the fixed structure.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'be designed in ways that make' correctly?
options: Some were designed in ways that make repair impossible without specialised tools.
Some were designed in ways what make repair impossible without specialised tools.
Some were designed in ways that makes repair impossible without specialised tools.
Some were designed in ways that to make repair impossible without specialised tools.
mcQuestions.explanation: Relative 'that' for things; 'ways' is plural so the verb 'make' takes no -s; no 'to' after 'that' in this position.
mcQuestions.hint: Relative 'that' (not 'what'); plural subject takes plural verb.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'is being made' (present continuous passive) correctly?
options: The decision is be made for us, slowly, by what we throw away.
The decision is being made for us, slowly, by what we throw away.
The decision is being make for us, slowly, by what we throw away.
The decision being made for us, slowly, by what we throw away.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present continuous passive = 'is/are being + past participle'.
mcQuestions.hint: Present continuous passive = 'is/are + being + past participle'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'cannot make … anymore' correctly?
options: A society that has lost those shops cannot makes the choice anymore.
A society that has lost those shops cannot to make the choice anymore.
A society that has lost those shops cannot make the choice anymore.
A society that has lost those shops cannot making the choice anymore.
mcQuestions.explanation: Modal + bare infinitive; 'anymore' goes at the end of the clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'show up on' correctly?
options: What closes with it will not show up at any inventory.
What closes with it will not show up in any inventory.
What closes with it will not show up for any inventory.
What closes with it will not show up on any inventory.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Show up on + a list/inventory' is the standard collocation in this metaphorical sense.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If the option to repair quietly disappears once enough small shops close, what would a society have to actively do — not just allow — to keep repair as a real choice for the next generation?
